did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model named MFA-MViT (based on MobileViT with multi-level feature fusion and attention mechanisms) to classify skin diseases. The task involves classifying dermoscopic and clinical images of skin lesions (such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) from the ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model, MFA-MViT, for skin disease classification. It details the methodology (multi-level feature fusion and attention mechanism), describes the construction of the ISIC-PAD dataset from ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, and reports quantitative experimental results (accuracy, precision, specificity, F1 score, FLOPs, parameters) from ablation studies and comparisons with baseline models. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICIBA 2023), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Classification Based on Multi-level Feature Fusion and Attention Mechanism
journal: 2023 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Information Technology, Big Data and Artificial Intelligence (ICIBA 2023)
brief_summary: This study proposes MFA-MViT, a lightweight skin disease classification model based on multi-level feature fusion and an attention mechanism, to address the computational constraints of mobile devices. The model integrates an inverted residual block with channel attention (Attn-DWS) and a multi-branch attention strategy to enhance feature extraction while reducing complexity. Evaluation was conducted on the ISIC-PAD dataset, constructed from the ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets, covering six categories of skin diseases including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The experimental results demonstrate that MFA-MViT achieves an accuracy of 92.56% and an F1 score of 92.54%, outperforming baseline models like MobileViT and other lightweight networks such as ShuffleNetV2 and MobileNetV2. Furthermore, the proposed method reduces computational cost by 16.16M FLOPs and parameters by 0.35M compared to the baseline, confirming its effectiveness for efficient clinical deployment.

all_task_types.target: Six categories of skin diseases (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using the MFA-MViT model on the ISIC-PAD dataset. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories from dermoscopic and clinical images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1 score, with a confusion matrix used to analyze per-class prediction performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 25,331 high-resolution dermoscopic images of skin diseases.

use.training: Source data for the constructed ISIC-PAD dataset.
use.other: Used as a source for the ISIC-PAD dataset; samples for BKL and DF were discarded during construction.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 2,298 clinical images collected by smartphones.

use.training: Source data for the constructed ISIC-PAD dataset.
use.other: Used as a source for the ISIC-PAD dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-PAD
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset constructed by combining ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, using random oversampling and instance hardness threshold undersampling to address class imbalance. It contains 18,000 images divided into six categories with 3,000 images per category.

use.training: Used for training the MFA-MViT model and conducting ablation experiments.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the MFA-MViT model and comparing it with baseline models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, precision, specificity, F1 score) and percentage point improvements without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-PAD.description: Custom dataset constructed from ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, containing 18,000 images across six categories (ACK, BCC, MEL, NEV, SCC, SEK) with 3,000 images per category.

ablation_experiments.metric: Accuracy (%), Precision (%), Specificity (%), F1 Score (%), FLOPs (M), Parameters (M)
ablation_experiments.notes: MFA-MViT improved accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1 score by 1.23%, 1.24%, 0.24%, and 1.22% respectively compared to the baseline MobileViT, while reducing computation by 16.16M FLOPs and parameters by 0.35M. The model showed significant improvement on the SEK category compared to MobileViT, which had poor accuracy on this class.

comparative_experiments.metric: Accuracy (%), Precision (%), Specificity (%), F1 Score (%), FLOPs (M), Parameters (M)
comparative_experiments.notes: MFA-MViT achieved the best results in accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1 score among the compared lightweight networks. However, it had a significantly higher computational cost (FLOPs) compared to ShuffleNetV2, MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, and EfficientNetV1, though fewer parameters than MobileViT.

architecture_used.main_model: MFA-MViT (MobileViT-based model with multi-level feature fusion and attention mechanism)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for MFA-MViT; baseline MobileViT is implied to be the starting point, but specific pretraining weights (e.g., ImageNet) are not detailed in the provided text for the proposed model.

modules.name: Attn-DWS module
modules.description: Linear bottleneck inverted residual block with local cross-channel interaction attention (ECA attention module). Uses depthwise separable convolution, global pooling, and adaptive kernel size 1D convolution for channel attention.

modules.name: Multi-branch attention mechanism
modules.description: Fuses features from multiple levels/stages to preserve shallow detailed features and supplement deep semantic features.

modules.name: Terminal EXP-MViT block
modules.description: Redesigned interaction at the end of the network. Uses 1x1 convolution instead of n x n convolution followed by 1x1 convolution to simplify learning and reduce parameters/FLOPs. Involves expanding convolution projection (d=240) and compressed convolution.

classification_or_output_head.type: Not explicitly reported (standard classification head implied)

preprocessing_techniques: Random oversampling combined with instance hardness threshold undersampling to address class imbalance in the constructed ISIC-PAD dataset
Discarding samples for classes (benign keratosis and dermatofibroma) not present in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset during dataset construction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used during the construction of the ISIC-PAD dataset to address class imbalance, combined with instance hardness threshold undersampling. Applied to balance the six categories (ACK, BCC, MEL, NEV, SCC, SEK) to 3,000 images per category.
Used in combination with random oversampling during the construction of the ISIC-PAD dataset to address class imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed MFA-MViT model, which integrates multi-level feature fusion and an attention mechanism, achieves superior classification performance compared to baseline models like MobileViT and other lightweight networks (ShuffleNetV2, MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV1) on the ISIC-PAD dataset.
MFA-MViT achieved an accuracy of 92.56% and an F1 score of 92.54%, representing improvements of 1.23% and 1.22% respectively over the MobileViT baseline.

The MFA-MViT model reduces computational cost by 16.16M FLOPs and parameters by 0.35M compared to the MobileViT baseline, demonstrating its efficiency for deployment on mobile or embedded devices with limited resources.
The model shows significant performance gains on specific classes, particularly seborrheic keratosis (SEK), which had poor accuracy in the baseline MobileViT model.

Ablation studies indicate that the terminal EXP-MViT block module provides the highest individual performance gain among the proposed components by simplifying the learning process and avoiding unnecessary channel compression.

The combination of the Attn-DWS module and multi-level feature fusion effectively extracts richer features and preserves shallow detailed features while supplementing deep semantic features, addressing the ambiguity of key position detail features in skin disease classification.
important_remarks: The study relies on a custom, constructed dataset (ISIC-PAD) created by combining ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, rather than using standard public splits, which limits direct comparability with other works using standard ISIC2019 splits.

Class imbalance was addressed via random oversampling and instance hardness threshold undersampling to create a perfectly balanced dataset (3,000 images per class), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence distributions.

Two classes present in ISIC2019 (benign keratosis and dermatofibroma) were excluded from the final dataset because they were absent in PAD-UFES-20, limiting the model's applicability to the full spectrum of skin diseases available in ISIC2019.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance improvements; comparisons are based solely on absolute metric differences.
The model was evaluated only on the constructed ISIC-PAD dataset; there is no external validation on independent, unseen datasets to assess generalizability.

The baseline MobileViT model showed notably poor accuracy on the seborrheic keratosis (SEK) class, which was significantly improved by MFA-MViT, suggesting the baseline's weakness may have inflated the perceived relative gain of the proposed method.
The paper does not report training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, loss function, training schedule), making it difficult to reproduce the results exactly.

The computational cost (FLOPs) of MFA-MViT (1420.0M) is significantly higher than other lightweight networks like ShuffleNetV2 (147.79M) and MobileNetV2 (312.92M), despite being lower than the MobileViT baseline (1436.16M), which may limit its suitability for resource-constrained mobile devices compared to other lightweight options.
The paper is published in a conference proceedings (ICIBA 2023) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
